author: @overbuiltautomotive1299
text: good job i love thing like this God bless Luke

author: @joshwhatley8516
text: Not to be a dick but spend a little more time on your miter and youll save yourself a ton of work man . I know from time to time you gotta bust out the old filler but i would keep in mind thats last resort strategy and by far the hardest way to achieve a suitable result. Sand a couple of those bad boys by hand and ye shall be converted.  Love the channel fellas keep it up.

author: @karlmyers6518
text: Nice Video. I felt i met the real Luke 5*

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thanks mate! :)

author: @jasonwitt8619
text: Good video mate but can I recommend something, a little trick I do is I use leftover wood saw dust to mix with epoxy when doing wood projects, the more fine it is the better. It comes out looking so perfect that you can't even see that a seam was even there, mainly because it is almost all wood. I do mix it thick just as you just showed. Cheers mate and thanks for the video.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Awesome tip! I might have to gove this ago, Thanks mate!

author: @stevenfaber3896
text: Is there a strength test on that angle coming? You should be able to setup 2 force meters or luggage weighing machines and go until it splits.

author: @tabbott429
text: Hmm do I have any zeolite laying around? Nope. I do have some actual body filler though. ;)

author: @Opel_Guy
text: I  used ordinary car body filler to repair damaged edges on a door frame a couple of months back prior to painting. Worked great! Easier to use than ordinary wood filler.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: hahaha awesome I would have done the same thing tbh. it is definitely easier lol.

text: Excellent video.

author: @alisonhenry820
text: Would this work as autobody filler?

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I don't know tbh, but I am going to use this to repair my front bumper on my 318i BMW which I will end up doing a video on if it works well.

author: @X02switchblades
text:
when filling a good thing is to spread some masking tape on both sides so that the joint is the only thing exposed 
Makes the clean-up a bit easier
Learned it from scale model making Cheers!

replies.author: @dr.lexwinter8604
replies.text: Maybe if you're making models that's an option. It's too fiddly, too costly, and too much work wasting your time and effort and overpriced masking tape when you're just going to hit the entire surface anyway with sandpaper.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Awesome tip! I will do this next time. It will defiantly save me a few minutes in sanding. Thanks mate! :)

author: @kalliste23
text: That's wood... have you never heard of sawdust and wood glue?

replies.author: @jamest.5001
replies.text: It works great! Nothing wrong with that!

replies.author: @jamest.5001
replies.text: It works out to be just like wood, epoxy may be harger than the wood, making sanding slightly difficult,  sawdust and glue, I keep a bit of my clean saw dust just incase,

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: Yours is a great suggestion when it comes to filling wood speficially, but the body filler is a better "all-purpose" solution to have handy around the shop and know how to make/use, which I think was the intent of his video.  I'm guessing the wood piece is simply what he had handy to demonstrate applying it.
